item.title: Cook
item.description: Tested recipes. Filter by cuisine, course, diet, time, and difficulty.

item.title: Explore
item.description: Three families gather every entry. Pick a family for a focused walk, or jump straight to a category.

item.title: Pantry
item.description: What ingredients are, how to choose and store them, what to swap. Plus the equipment that earns its drawer.

item.title: Skills
item.description: Methods that show up across many recipes. Cuisines built from a pantry. Curated reading paths.

item.title: Weeknight pasta
item.description: Pasta dishes that hit the table in under 30 minutes without sacrificing technique.

item.title: All-purpose flour
item.description: Wheat flour at 10–12% protein. The middle of the road — strong enough for bread in a pinch, soft enough for cake if you handle it gently.

item.title: Assam tea
item.description: Robust, malty black tea from northeast India. The default base for masala chai and what gives English Breakfast its backbone.

item.title: Avocado oil
item.description: Pressed from avocado flesh, mostly monounsaturated fat. Holds high heat without smoking and stays neutral enough for searing where olive oil's fruit notes would intrude.

item.title: Baking powder
item.description: A pre-mixed chemical leavener: baking soda plus one or two dry acids plus a starch buffer. Double-acting versions release CO2 in two stages — once when wet, again at oven heat.

item.title: Baking soda
item.description: Pure sodium bicarbonate. An alkali that needs an acid in the batter to release CO2 and lift — and that simultaneously raises pH, deepening browning.

item.title: Black peppercorns
item.description: Dried unripe fruit of Piper nigrum. Heat from piperine; aroma from terpenes that vanish on grinding.

item.title: Butter (unsalted)
item.description: Cow's-milk fat with ~16% water and ~2% milk solids. The solids are the flavor lever in cooking.

item.title: Chili oil (辣椒油)
item.description: Neutral oil infused with dried chilies and aromatics, with the toasted chili sediment retained. Both the clear oil and the sediment carry distinct functions in a dish.

item.title: Chinkiang black vinegar (镇江香醋)
item.description: Aged glutinous-rice vinegar from Zhenjiang. Dark, malty, smoky, and only mildly acidic — closer in spirit to balsamic than to white vinegar.

item.title: Cinnamon stick
item.description: Dried inner bark of Cinnamomum trees. Two main types: hot, sharp Cassia (the supermarket default) and floral, gentler Ceylon ("true" cinnamon).

item.title: Dark brown sugar
item.description: Granulated sugar coated with ~6.5% molasses — about double the light-brown ratio. More acid, more spread, more Maillard, deeper flavor.

item.title: Dark chocolate
item.description: Cocoa solids and cocoa butter combined with sugar, with no milk solids. The cacao percentage on the label is solids + butter together, not solids alone.

item.title: Dark soy sauce (老抽)
item.description: Aged longer than light soy and cut with molasses or caramel. Used in small amounts for color and depth, not for primary salting.

item.title: Dried osmanthus (桂花)
item.description: Dried flowers of Osmanthus fragrans — tiny golden florets used as a floral garnish and tea-blender. Carries a stone-fruit and apricot character from γ-decalactone.

item.title: Dutch-process cocoa
item.description: Cocoa powder treated with potassium carbonate to neutralize its natural acidity. Darker, mellower, and chemically incompatible with recipes built around natural cocoa.

item.title: Eggs (large)
item.description: Roughly 50g per large egg: ~30g white, ~18g yolk, ~6g shell. The white sets at 62°C, the yolk at 70°C — that gap is half of what you cook with.

item.title: Evaporated milk
item.description: Milk reduced by ~60% with no added sugar, sterilized in the can. Adds dairy body without the sweetness load of condensed milk.

item.title: Fine sea salt
item.description: Evaporated seawater, ground fine. Small crystals dissolve fast and distribute evenly — the right salt for baking and for anything that needs to disappear.

item.title: Garlic
item.description: Allium sativum. Pungency comes from allicin, which only forms when you cut or crush a clove — and which mellows quickly with heat.

item.title: Ginger (fresh)
item.description: Rhizome of Zingiber officinale. Sharp, peppery heat from gingerol when raw; mellower and sweeter (zingerone) when cooked.

item.title: Granulated sugar
item.description: Refined sucrose, medium crystal. Does four jobs in baking — aerates, structures, browns, and holds water — and it's the default for a reason.

item.title: Heavy cream
item.description: Cow's cream at ≥36% milkfat. The fat content is what lets it whip and what keeps it stable when reduced.

item.title: Kosher salt
item.description: Coarse-grain sodium chloride, no iodine, no anti-caking agents. Cooking salt of choice because the crystal shape distributes evenly by hand.

item.title: Light brown sugar
item.description: Granulated sugar coated with ~3.5% molasses. The molasses adds moisture, acid, and color — and changes how a cookie spreads.

item.title: Light soy sauce (生抽)
item.description: The everyday Chinese seasoning soy — saltier, thinner, and lighter in color than dark soy. This is what most stir-fries and dipping sauces actually want.
